attributes.stem:
A 79-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with his son due to concerns about his memory. He has a medical history of hypothyroidism, osteoarthritis and hypertension. His medications include thyroxine, perindopril, ibuprofen as needed and paracetamol as needed.

Over the past 12 months, he has missed several doctors' appointments and his son's recent birthday dinner, despite reminders. His son reports that his father often has word-finding difficulty in conversation and finds himself prompting his father. The patient states he is still able to perform tasks around the home such as cooking and cleaning, but they sometimes take longer if he misplaces an item. His son agrees that his father still manages normal household tasks including cooking, cleaning and paying bills. The patient is aware his memory is not what it once was, but feels he is coping fine on his own at home.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. His Mini-Mental State Examination score is 26/30.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The most likely diagnosis for this patient is Mild cognitive impairment (MCI). MCI is characterised by memory decline worse than expected for a patient's age, however, they do not meet the criteria for diagnosis of dementia and are still able to perform activities of daily living (ADLs) independently and maintain social functioning. This diagnosis best fits this patient's presentation as he is still managing on his own at home. While MCI indicates worsened memory, it does not necessarily mean a patient will develop dementia, although they are at greater risk and therefore should be followed up to ensure patient safety.

Depression with pseudodementia can present with cognitive symptoms similar to dementia, including memory problems and difficulty concentrating. However, this patient does not show typical signs of depression such as low mood, loss of interest in activities, or changes in sleep or appetite. The cognitive symptoms are the primary concern, making MCI more likely.

Normal ageing involves some slowing of cognitive processes and mild forgetfulness, but it does not significantly impact daily functioning or social interactions. This patient's symptoms, including missing appointments and having word-finding difficulties, are more pronounced than what would be expected in normal ageing, making MCI a more appropriate diagnosis.

Alzheimer's dementia is characterised by progressive cognitive decline that interferes with daily functioning and independence. Although this patient has decline in the memory domain and is in the typical age range for Alzheimer's, he does not yet meet the criteria for dementia as his cognitive deficits do not significantly interfere with everyday life and he still maintains his independence at this stage.

Personality disorder is a long-standing pattern of maladaptive thoughts, feelings, and behaviours that significantly impacts a person's life. While personality changes can occur with cognitive decline, there is no evidence in this case to suggest a longstanding pattern of personality issues. The patient's symptoms are primarily cognitive in nature, and the onset is recent, which is inconsistent with a personality disorder diagnosis. Furthermore, personality disorders typically manifest earlier in life and are not commonly diagnosed for the first time in older adults.

The key to differentiating MCI from dementia is the preservation of functional independence. While this patient shows clear cognitive decline, particularly in memory, he is still able to manage his daily activities independently, albeit with some inefficiency. His MMSE score of 26/30 also supports MCI rather than dementia, as it indicates mild cognitive impairment but not severe enough to meet dementia criteria. Regular follow-up is important for patients with MCI due to the increased risk of progression to dementia.
learningPoints: Mild Cognitive Impairment presents with memory decline worse than expected for age, but patients retain independence with activities of daily living and have normal social functioning.
Mild Cognitive Impairment is distinguished from dementia by preserved functional independence.
Regular follow-up is important for patients with Mild Cognitive Impairment due to increased risk of progression to dementia.
references: Scott KR, Barrett AM. Dementia syndromes: evaluation and treatment. Expert review of neurotherapeutics. 2007;7(4):407-22. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2536654/

Larson EV. Evaluation of cognitive impairment and dementia. In: Uptodate [online]. 2019 [cited 2021 Sep 7]. Available at: https://www.uptodate.com/contents/evaluation-of-cognitive-impairment-and-dementia?search=dementia+types&source=search_result&selectedTitle=1~150&usage_type=default&display_rank=1#H1199441673

Wolk DA, Dickerson BC. Clinical features and diagnosis of Alzheimer disease. In: Uptodate [online]. 2020 [cited 2021 Sep 7]. Available at: https://www.uptodate.com/contents/clinical-features-and-diagnosis-of-alzheimer-disease?search=alzheimrs%20and%20vascular%20dementia&source=search_result&selectedTitle=1~150&usage_type=default&display_rank=1#H24

Moreira HS, Costa AS, Machado Á, Castro SL, Lima CF, Vicente SG. Distinguishing mild cognitive impairment from healthy aging and Alzheimer's Disease: The contribution of the INECO Frontal Screening (IFS). PLoS One. 2019;14(9):e0221873. Published 2019 Sep 10. doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0221873
